title: Week 2 Worklog
content:
Week 2 Objectives: Account setup and cost management strategies Identity and access management best practices Network architecture with VPC design patterns Compute services deployment and optimization Storage solutions and database implementations Monitoring and observability frameworks Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 1 - AWS IAM: Configure administrative groups, policies, and roles.
- Amazon VPC: Build a secure virtual network including subnets, route tables, Internet/NAT Gateways, Security Groups, NACLs, Site-to-Site VPN, and Flow Logs.
- Amazon EC2: Deploy Linux/Windows VM instances, modify instance types, manage custom AMIs/EBS snapshots, and apply region/family governance restrictions. 04/27/2026 04/27/2026 https://000002.awsstudygroup.com/
https://000003.awsstudygroup.com/
https://000004.awsstudygroup.com/ 2 - IAM Role on EC2: Grant secure, credential-less access to AWS services (S3) using EC2 IAM Roles.
- Amazon S3 & CloudFront: Host a static website, set up CloudFront distribution with Origin Access Control (OAC), enable S3 versioning, and configure Cross-Region Replication (CRR). 04/28/2026 04/28/2026 https://000048.awsstudygroup.com/
https://000057.awsstudygroup.com/ 3 - Amazon RDS: Provision relational databases with DB Subnet Groups, Multi-AZ high availability (synchronous replication), Read Replicas (asynchronous scaling), and backup/restore mechanisms.
- Amazon Lightsail: Deploy cost-efficient application stacks (WordPress, Akaunting) and databases, configure firewalls (SSH port 22), snapshots, and resource alarms.
- EC2 Autoscaling: Create custom AMIs, Launch Templates, Target Groups, Application Load Balancers, and set up Auto Scaling Groups validated via Paessler WebStress load testing. 04/29/2026 04/29/2026 https://000005.awsstudygroup.com/
https://000045.awsstudygroup.com/
https://000006.awsstudygroup.com/ 4 - Amazon CloudWatch: Monitor infrastructure using custom metrics, centralized log groups, dynamic alarms, and unified dashboards.
- Amazon Route 53: Build hybrid DNS connectivity using Inbound/Outbound Resolver Endpoints and forwarding Rules. 04/30/2026 04/30/2026 https://000008.awsstudygroup.com/
https://000010.awsstudygroup.com/ 5 - AWS CLI: Configure and use the AWS CLI on remote compute instances (Windows/Ubuntu), manage CLI profiles, customize output formats, and parse results using shell utilities (grep, awk, sed). 04/31/2026 04/31/2026 https://000011.awsstudygroup.com/ Week 2 Achievements: Access Management (AWS IAM)
Configured detailed access controls for AWS resources using IAM Groups, Users, and Customer Managed Policies. Implemented secure temporary credentials and delegated access using IAM Roles rather than permanent security keys. Applied the principle of least privilege across administrative and user-level permissions. 💡 Pro Tips & Notes: Always use IAM Roles for temporary permissions and set session timeouts to limit exposure. Monitor identity access and API activities continuously using AWS CloudTrail. Secure Application Access (IAM Roles on EC2)
Replaced hardcoded IAM Access Keys and Secret Access Keys in application code with temporary credentials from an IAM Role. Provisioned an EC2 instance and S3 bucket to test secure, credential-less programmatic uploads. 💡 Pro Tips & Notes: Hardcoded credentials pose a high security risk. Always assign IAM Roles to EC2 instances for secure S3/AWS service access. Static Website Hosting (Amazon S3 & CloudFront)
Configured Amazon S3 static website hosting, utilizing S3 Standard storage with 99.999999999% durability. Integrated S3 hosting with Amazon CloudFront using Origin Access Control (OAC) to secure the origin bucket. Enabled S3 Versioning to preserve historical object states and configured Cross-Region Replication (CRR) from Singapore for geographic redundancy. 💡 Pro Tips & Notes: Keep S3 Block Public Access enabled and route traffic exclusively through CloudFront to enforce HTTPS and caching. Use S3 Lifecycle Policies to automatically transition or expire old object versions to control storage costs. Network Infrastructure (Amazon VPC)
Designed and provisioned custom VPCs with public subnets (routed to IGW) and private subnets (routed to NAT Gateway) across multiple AZs. Configured stateful Security Groups (instance-level) and stateless Network ACLs (subnet-level) for multi-layered network security. Configured Site-to-Site VPN with dual redundant IPSec tunnels and enabled VPC Flow Logs for traffic analysis. 💡 Pro Tips & Notes: AWS reserves 5 IP addresses in each subnet (first 4 and last 1) for internal networking. Use NAT Gateways instead of NAT Instances in production for automatic scaling and lower administrative overhead. Compute Services (Amazon EC2)
Provisioned and connected to Windows Server 2025 (via RDP) and Amazon Linux 2023 (via SSH/Session Manager). Deployed a Node.js CRUD User Management application under LAMP (Linux) and XAMPP (Windows) environments. Created EBS snapshots, custom AMIs, and applied IAM governance policies to restrict EC2 regions, instance types, and volume categories. 💡 Pro Tips & Notes: Terminate unused instances and release associated Elastic IPs to prevent unexpected charges. Use AWS Systems Manager Session Manager for secure instance access without opening inbound SSH/RDP ports. Managed Databases (Amazon RDS)
Selected managed database engines (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Aurora) in Multi-AZ DB Subnet Groups. Configured Multi-AZ synchronous replication with automatic failover (60-120 seconds) and asynchronous Read Replicas for read-heavy workloads. Established backup management using automated point-in-time recovery (up to 35 days) and manual DB snapshots. 💡 Pro Tips & Notes: Automated snapshots are performed on standby Multi-AZ instances to prevent primary database latency. To encrypt an existing unencrypted database, copy its snapshot, apply KMS encryption to the copy, and restore from it. Compute Cost Optimization (Amazon Lightsail)
Provisioned simplified, low-cost application stacks (WordPress, Akaunting) and high-availability database instances on Lightsail. Minimized host attack surface by removing port 22 (SSH) from both IPv4 and IPv6 instance firewalls. Configured manual snapshots, automatic daily snapshots, and resource alarms (e.g. CPU utilization). 💡 Pro Tips & Notes: Manual snapshots are persistent and not automatically deleted when the parent instance is terminated; clean them up manually to avoid storage costs. Application Autoscaling (EC2 Auto Scaling & ELB)
Created custom AMIs and configured Launch Templates to boot uniform application servers across multiple AZs. Provisioned Application Load Balancers (ALBs) and Target Groups, integrating them with Auto Scaling Groups (ASGs). Evaluated scaling strategies (Manual, Scheduled, Dynamic, Predictive) and validated dynamic scaling by load testing with Paessler WebStress. 💡 Pro Tips & Notes: Integrate ELB with AWS Certificate Manager (ACM) to offload SSL/TLS encryption (SSL termination) from target servers. Combine predictive scaling with target tracking dynamic scaling to proactively handle cyclical traffic spikes. Observability (Amazon CloudWatch)
Collected performance metrics and centralized application/system logs under CloudWatch Log Groups. Created custom metric alarms to trigger automated notifications and instance recovery actions. Built unified CloudWatch Dashboards to monitor overall application and infrastructure health. 💡 Pro Tips & Notes: Enable Container Insights to automatically collect and aggregate compute metrics (CPU, memory, disk, network) for ECS, EKS, and Fargate. Hybrid DNS Integration (Amazon Route 53)
Explored Route 53 Resolver capabilities including private hosted zones (PHZ) and recursive query resolution. Configured Inbound Endpoints (resolving AWS domains from local) and Outbound Endpoints (resolving local domains from AWS). Created Resolver Rules to conditionally forward queries for specific domain namespaces to local DNS servers. 💡 Pro Tips & Notes: Distribute DNS endpoints across multiple Availability Zones to prevent single points of failure in DNS routing. Command Line Administration (AWS CLI)
Configured the AWS CLI with default/named profiles on remote EC2 instances. Executed API operations and parsed text-based output using Unix utilities (grep, awk, sed). 💡 Pro Tips & Notes: Use the ’text’ output format in shell scripts for easy integration and processing with command-line utilities. Always use IAM Roles instead of static credentials (access keys) to grant secure access.

title: Overview & Architecture
content:
1. Idea & Objectives Context & Problem Small and medium businesses (SMEs) need an HR system (employees, departments, payroll data) but do not want to run and maintain their own servers. A single shared SaaS must keep each company’s data strictly separated.
Target Customer SME companies that subscribe to the SaaS to manage HR. Each company = one isolated tenant (data isolation enforced per request via tenant_id).
Goals Deliver a multi-tenant HR SaaS on AWS with tenant data isolation. Highly available (Multi-AZ) and horizontally scalable application tier. Managed, low-ops services (no self-managed servers). Success Criteria End-to-end login → authenticated API → data read/write works. Async event (tenant → HR) delivered via SQS. Survives an AZ / RDS failure (Multi-AZ failover). Monthly cost kept ~$130 (24/7), near $0 when torn down. Fit with the program Real-world AWS use-case using ≥3 managed services — covers compute, data, identity, messaging, edge, and monitoring.
2. Architecture Description Architecture Diagram Three tiers inside one VPC across two Availability Zones:
Presentation: CloudFront (edge) → S3 (private static, OAC). Application: ALB (public) → ECS Fargate (private) running auth / tenant / hr. Data: RDS MySQL Multi-AZ (private). Async via SQS; auth via app-signed RS256 JWT. AWS Services Used Layer Service Role Edge CloudFront CDN + single entry (static + API) Static S3 (private + OAC) React build artifacts Identity App-signed RS256 JWT auth-service signs JWT (\tenant_id + role claims); tenant/hr verify via public key. Cognito pool provisioned, not wired in. Load balancing Application Load Balancer Path-routing /api/v1/* to ECS Compute ECS Fargate (3 services) auth / tenant / hr microservices Registry ECR Container images Database RDS MySQL db.t4g.micro Multi-AZ Primary + Standby, 3 logical DBs Async SQS (+ DLQ) tenant → queue → hr Secrets SSM Parameter Store DB password, SQS URL, JWT keys, Cognito IDs Monitoring CloudWatch + SNS Logs + CPU alarm → email Network VPC, IGW, 1 NAT Gateway 6 subnets / 2 AZ Why these services (rationale) Service Why chosen ECS Fargate Serverless containers — no EC2 to patch/scale-manage; native auto scaling. RDS Multi-AZ Managed HA database with synchronous standby + automatic failover + backups. SQS Decouples tenant→hr, absorbs spikes, survives consumer outage; DLQ for poison messages; no broker to run. App-signed RS256 JWT auth-service = single issuer (private key); tenant/hr = stateless verifiers (public key). No shared secret, no external IdP. (Cognito pool provisioned but not in the flow.) CloudFront + S3 Cheap, edge-cached static hosting; S3 stays private via OAC. ALB L7 path-routing to 3 services + health checks. SSM Parameter Store Free SecureString secrets — no hard-coded keys in images.

Event 1
content:
⚠️ Note: The information below is for reference purposes only. Please do not copy it verbatim into your report, including this warning.
Summary Report: “GenAI-powered App-DB Modernization workshop” Event Objectives Share best practices in modern application design Introduce Domain-Driven Design (DDD) and event-driven architecture Provide guidance on selecting the right compute services Present AI tools to support the development lifecycle Speakers Jignesh Shah – Director, Open Source Databases Erica Liu – Sr. GTM Specialist, AppMod Fabrianne Effendi – Assc. Specialist SA, Serverless Amazon Web Services Key Highlights Identifying the drawbacks of legacy application architecture Long product release cycles → Lost revenue/missed opportunities Inefficient operations → Reduced productivity, higher costs Non-compliance with security regulations → Security breaches, loss of reputation Transitioning to modern application architecture – Microservices Migrating to a modular system — each function is an independent service communicating via events, built on three core pillars:
Queue Management: Handle asynchronous tasks Caching Strategy: Optimize performance Message Handling: Flexible inter-service communication Domain-Driven Design (DDD) Four-step method: Identify domain events → arrange timeline → identify actors → define bounded contexts Bookstore case study: Demonstrates real-world DDD application Context mapping: 7 patterns for integrating bounded contexts Event-Driven Architecture 3 integration patterns: Publish/Subscribe, Point-to-point, Streaming Benefits: Loose coupling, scalability, resilience Sync vs async comparison: Understanding the trade-offs Compute Evolution Shared Responsibility Model: EC2 → ECS → Fargate → Lambda Serverless benefits: No server management, auto-scaling, pay-for-value Functions vs Containers: Criteria for appropriate choice Amazon Q Developer SDLC automation: From planning to maintenance Code transformation: Java upgrade, .NET modernization AWS Transform agents: VMware, Mainframe, .NET migration Key Takeaways Design Mindset Business-first approach: Always start from the business domain, not the technology Ubiquitous language: Importance of a shared vocabulary between business and tech teams Bounded contexts: Identifying and managing complexity in large systems Technical Architecture Event storming technique: Practical method for modeling business processes Use event-driven communication instead of synchronous calls Integration patterns: When to use sync, async, pub/sub, streaming Compute spectrum: Criteria for choosing between VM, containers, and serverless Modernization Strategy Phased approach: No rushing — follow a clear roadmap 7Rs framework: Multiple modernization paths depending on the application ROI measurement: Cost reduction + business agility Applying to Work Apply DDD to current projects: Event storming sessions with business teams Refactor microservices: Use bounded contexts to define service boundaries Implement event-driven patterns: Replace some sync calls with async messaging Adopt serverless: Pilot AWS Lambda for suitable use cases Try Amazon Q Developer: Integrate into the dev workflow to boost productivity Event Experience Attending the “GenAI-powered App-DB Modernization” workshop was extremely valuable, giving me a comprehensive view of modernizing applications and databases using advanced methods and tools. Key experiences included:
Learning from highly skilled speakers Experts from AWS and major tech organizations shared best practices in modern application design. Through real-world case studies, I gained a deeper understanding of applying DDD and Event-Driven Architecture to large projects. Hands-on technical exposure Participating in event storming sessions helped me visualize how to model business processes into domain events. Learned how to split microservices and define bounded contexts to manage large-system complexity. Understood trade-offs between synchronous and asynchronous communication and integration patterns like pub/sub, point-to-point, streaming. Leveraging modern tools Explored Amazon Q Developer, an AI tool for SDLC support from planning to maintenance. Learned to automate code transformation and pilot serverless with AWS Lambda to improve productivity. Networking and discussions The workshop offered opportunities to exchange ideas with experts, peers, and business teams, enhancing the ubiquitous language between business and tech. Real-world examples reinforced the importance of the business-first approach rather than focusing solely on technology. Lessons learned Applying DDD and event-driven patterns reduces coupling while improving scalability and resilience. Modernization requires a phased approach with ROI measurement; rushing the process can be risky. AI tools like Amazon Q Developer can significantly boost productivity when integrated into the current workflow. Some event photos Add your event photos here
Overall, the event not only provided technical knowledge but also helped me reshape my thinking about application design, system modernization, and cross-team collaboration.

title: Week 3 Worklog
content:
Week 3 Objectives: Workload assessment and migration planning Server and application migration strategies Database migration with minimal downtime Network connectivity during migration phases Post-migration optimization and validation Risk mitigation and rollback procedures Operational Excellence: Automation, monitoring, and incident response Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 1 - AWS VM Import/Export: Study virtual server migration concepts, exporting local VMs (VMware/VirtualBox) to S3, and importing them into Amazon Machine Images (AMI). 05/04/2026 05/04/2026 https://000014.awsstudygroup.com/ 2 - AWS Lambda Cost Optimization: Learn serverless automation concepts for EC2 startup/shutdown schedules and Slack webhook notification integrations. 05/05/2026 05/05/2026 https://000022.awsstudygroup.com/ 3 - CloudWatch & Grafana Monitoring: Research system monitoring, metric aggregation using CloudWatch, and dashboard visualization with Grafana. 05/06/2026 05/06/2026 https://000029.awsstudygroup.com/ 4 - AWS Tags & Resource Groups: Study metadata tagging standards, query-based resource grouping, and attribute-based access control (ABAC) in IAM. 05/07/2026 05/07/2026 https://000027.awsstudygroup.com/ https://000028.awsstudygroup.com/ 5 - AWS Systems Manager (SSM): Learn patch management automation (Patch Manager), bulk command execution (Run Command), and secure console sessions (Session Manager). 05/08/2026 05/08/2026 https://000031.awsstudygroup.com/ https://000058.awsstudygroup.com/ 6 - AWS CloudFormation & Cloud9: Research Infrastructure as Code (IaC) principles, template authoring (YAML/JSON), and Custom Resources using Lambda. 05/08/2026 05/08/2026 https://000037.awsstudygroup.com/ Week 3 Achievements: Migrate Virtual Servers with AWS VM Import/Export
Studied the concept, use cases, and prerequisites of VM Import/Export for migrating on-premises virtual machines to EC2. Explored VM image formats (OVA, VMDK, VHD) and the role of Amazon S3 as an intermediate storage layer. Understood the process of importing VM files into AMIs and exporting EC2 instances back to on-premises environments. Cost Optimization with AWS Lambda & Slack Integration
Studied serverless computing concepts and how to optimize EC2 running costs by automating server start/stop schedules. Understood long-term cost reduction strategies on AWS, including when to purchase Savings Plans for 24/7 workloads. Explored integrating AWS Lambda with Slack Incoming Webhooks to publish automated operation alerts. System Monitoring with Amazon CloudWatch & Grafana
Researched performance monitoring and centralizing system logs using CloudWatch Log Groups. Explored integrating CloudWatch with Grafana to construct unified operational dashboards. Resource Governance & Access Control (Tagging, Resource Groups, IAM)
Studied AWS Tags as metadata key-value pairs to identify, categorize, and organize AWS resources. Explored AWS Resource Groups to group resources in the same region using tag-based or CloudFormation stack-based queries. Learned the principle of IAM least privilege and how to write conditional statements (IAM Policy Conditions) using resource tags. Systems Management & Remote Access (AWS Systems Manager - SSM)
Researched automating OS patching and software updates on managed nodes using SSM Patch Manager. Studied bulk remote administration capabilities using SSM Run Command. Understood how SSM Session Manager provides secure, port-free console sessions without bastion hosts or SSH keys. Explored private connectivity to Systems Manager via VPC Interface Endpoints and auditing session commands using S3 logs. Infrastructure as Code (AWS CloudFormation & Cloud9)
Researched Infrastructure as Code (IaC) principles to declare, model, and deploy cloud resources programmatically. Explored AWS Cloud9 as a browser-based IDE for authoring and testing templates. Studied Custom Resources using AWS Lambda, focusing on how to automate operations like SSH key generation and SSM storage.

title: Week 4 Worklog
content:
Week 4 Objectives: Security: Zero-trust architecture, compliance, and threat protection Reliability: High availability, disaster recovery, and fault tolerance Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 1 - AWS IAM Identity Center & Permission Boundaries: Study AWS Organizations landing zone design, multi-account single sign-on (SSO), and delegating administration with IAM permission boundaries. 05/11/2026 05/11/2026 https://000012.awsstudygroup.com/ https://000030.awsstudygroup.com/ 2 - Role Transfer & Compliance: Study limiting role assumption using IAM policy condition keys and monitoring security posture compliance using AWS Security Hub. 05/12/2026 05/12/2026 https://000044.awsstudygroup.com/ https://000018.awsstudygroup.com/ 3 - Application Protection & Cryptography: Study web application defense using AWS WAF and encryption key lifecycle management using AWS KMS. 05/13/2026 05/13/2026 https://000026.awsstudygroup.com/ https://000033.awsstudygroup.com/ 4 - Data Protection & Private Networking: Study centralized data backup policies using AWS Backup and secure inter-VPC routing using VPC Peering. 05/14/2026 05/15/2026 https://000013.awsstudygroup.com/ https://000019.awsstudygroup.com/ 5 - Centralized Multi-Account Networking: Study cross-account network orchestration and transit routing configurations using AWS Transit Gateway. 05/15/2026 05/15/2026 https://000020.awsstudygroup.com/ Week 4 Achievements: Centralized Identity & Access Management (AWS IAM Identity Center & Permission Boundaries)
Learned to design landing zones using AWS Organizations and set up single sign-on (SSO) with IAM Identity Center to centrally manage multi-account access. Learned to use IAM Permission Boundaries to restrict maximum permissions, preventing privilege escalation for delegated administrators. Explored automated user management using Identity Store APIs and time-bound permission sets. Conditional IAM Role Assumption & Trust Policies
Learned the mechanics of IAM role trust relationships and how to configure secure delegation using the sts:AssumeRole API. Achieved granular security controls by restricting role switching through policy condition keys like IP range (aws:SourceIp) and time windows (aws:CurrentTime). Cloud Security Posture Management (AWS Security Hub & Config)
Learned to centralize security compliance tracking using AWS Security Hub, leveraging AWS Config recording rules to continuously audit resources. Achieved the ability to evaluate compliance against standards (e.g., CIS Benchmarks) and configure custom rule exclusions. Web Application Security (AWS WAF)
Learned to defend web applications against OWASP Top 10 exploits by configuring Web ACLs using a mix of managed and custom logical rules. Learned to analyze traffic and validate rules using non-terminating Count actions and logging configuration with Kinesis Firehose. Data Encryption & Key Management (AWS KMS & S3)
Learned the fundamentals of AWS KMS key management, key policy authorization, and how to protect data at rest on Amazon S3 (SSE-KMS). Achieved secure key lifecycle practices, key rotation, temporary data delegation using pre-signed URLs, and audit logging with CloudTrail and Athena. Centralized Data Protection & Disaster Recovery (AWS Backup)
Learned to implement automated, policy-based backup strategies across multi-service AWS resources using AWS Backup. Achieved disaster recovery preparation based on RTO/RPO requirements, Backup Vault encryption policies, and event-driven SNS alerts. Inter-VPC Private Routing (VPC Peering)
Learned to establish secure, private, and low-latency network connections between VPCs using VPC Peering. Achieved cross-VPC communication control using stateless Network ACLs, Route Table configurations, and Cross-VPC DNS resolution. Centralized Multi-Account Networking (AWS Transit Gateway)
Learned to deploy Transit Gateway as a centralized cloud router, simplifying network topologies compared to complex mesh VPC Peering. Achieved custom routing control using Transit Gateway Route Tables, route associations, propagations, and VPC-level route modifications.

title: Week 5 Worklog
content:
Week 5 Objectives: Containerization and application deployment strategies Continuous integration and delivery (CI/CD) pipelines Hybrid cloud storage and centralized repositories Data lake architecture and NoSQL database modeling Cost optimization models and compute resource right-sizing Cost visualization, data cataloging, and analytical query execution Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 1 - Deploy Application on Docker & Amazon ECS: Study containerization concepts, deployment workflows, and ECS container orchestration (Clusters, Tasks, Services, Load Balancing, Auto-Scaling, and Deployment Strategies). 05/18/2026 05/18/2026 https://000015.awsstudygroup.com/ https://000016.awsstudygroup.com/ 2 - AWS CodePipeline Automation: Study continuous integration and continuous delivery (CI/CD) concepts and automation pipelines using AWS CodePipeline. 05/19/2026 05/19/2026 https://000017.awsstudygroup.com/ https://000023.awsstudygroup.com/ 3 - AWS Storage Gateway & FSx: Study hybrid cloud storage solutions using File Storage Gateway and high-performance file sharing using Amazon FSx. 05/20/2026 05/20/2026 https://000024.awsstudygroup.com/ https://000025.awsstudygroup.com/ 4 - Data Lake & DynamoDB Architecture: Study the principles of building a Data Lake on AWS and advanced database modeling using Amazon DynamoDB. 05/21/2026 05/21/2026 https://000035.awsstudygroup.com/ https://000039.awsstudygroup.com/ 5 - AWS Cost Optimization: Study AWS Savings Plans, Reserved Instances, Reserved DB Instances, and EC2 resource optimization techniques. 05/22/2026 05/22/2026 https://000042.awsstudygroup.com/ https://000032.awsstudygroup.com/ 6 - Cost Visualization & Analytics: Study AWS Cost Explorer, usage visualization, and advanced cost analysis using AWS Glue and Amazon Athena. 05/23/2026 05/23/2026 https://000034.awsstudygroup.com/ https://000040.awsstudygroup.com/ Week 5 Achievements: Containerized Deployment with Docker & AWS Services
Studied the core containerization components including Docker Hub, Nginx reverse proxy, Amazon ECR, Amazon RDS, and Amazon EC2. Explored the client-to-database workflow: traffic routing via Internet Gateway and Nginx, frontend/backend container interaction, and RDS data persistence. Researched local container deployment methodologies compared to running containers on cloud virtual servers (EC2). Studied RDS capabilities such as DB subnet groups, Multi-AZ deployments, read replicas, and automatic storage scaling. Understood application orchestration using Docker Compose and the process of pushing/pulling images from Amazon ECR and Docker Hub. Container Orchestration with Amazon Elastic Container Service (ECS)
Researched Amazon ECS architecture and key components: Clusters, Task Definitions, Services, and container hosts (EC2 vs. Fargate). Studied Task Definitions as blueprints defining Docker image, CPU/memory allocations, network mode, log configurations, and execution IAM roles. Explored resource and application scaling mechanisms: Cluster Scaling (scaling instances/Fargate resources) and Service Scaling (adjusting running container tasks via CloudWatch). Studied application update strategies: Rolling Deployments (gradual container replacement) and Blue/Green Deployments (parallel environments with traffic shifting via CodeDeploy and ALB). Researched infrastructure preparations including CodeDeploy execution roles, NAT Gateways, Route Tables, and Application Load Balancers (ALB) for frontend/backend service routing. Continuous Integration, Delivery, and Monitoring (CI/CD, Container Insights, FireLens)
Studied the core concepts of Continuous Integration (CI) and Continuous Delivery (CD) to automate code building, testing, and deployment. Compared CI/CD workflows and automation pipelines across GitHub Actions, GitLab CI/CD, and AWS CodePipeline (integrating CodeBuild and CodeDeploy). Understood pipeline automation stages: triggering via git tags, building Docker images, pushing to ECR/Docker Hub, updating Task Definitions, and updating ECS Services (via CodeDeploy blue/green or rolling). Researched automated application deployment to Amazon EC2 instances using the AWS native developer suite (CodePipeline, CodeBuild, and CodeDeploy). Studied CodeCommit’s lifecycle status (deprecated for new customers as of July 2024) and the procedures for migrating repository data to other SCM providers (GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket) using git mirroring techniques. Understood the roles of AWS CodeBuild in compiling and testing code on dynamic build servers, and AWS CodeDeploy (leveraging CodeDeploy Agents on EC2) in automating application installation. Explored secure pipeline artifact storage on Amazon S3 with encryption at rest managed by AWS Key Management Service (KMS) keys. Researched container performance monitoring using Amazon CloudWatch Container Insights to track CPU, memory, network utilization, and task replica states. Studied log management and routing architectures using AWS FireLens to direct container logs to external targets like Amazon S3 and CloudWatch Logs. Hybrid Storage & File Sharing (AWS Storage Gateway & FSx)
Studied AWS Storage Gateway architecture, focusing on how File Storage Gateway acts as a bridge to synchronize data between on-premises environments and Amazon S3. Researched virtual deployment configurations of the Storage Gateway appliance on virtualization platforms (VMware ESXi, Microsoft Hyper-V, Linux KVM) or using pre-configured EC2 AMIs. Understood the process of creating and configuring File Shares (NFS/SMB) and mounting them to local or on-premises machines to enable unlimited cloud-backed storage integration. Researched Amazon FSx for Windows File Server architecture, including EC2 file servers using the SMB protocol, S3 data replication, VPC isolation, and ENI-based networking. Studied domain integration via Windows Active Directory and the setup of highly available file systems using SSD or HDD Multi-AZ configurations. Explored advanced file server administration features: data deduplication, shadow copies (VSS), user session management, storage quotas, and Continuous Access (CA) file shares. Data Lake Building & NoSQL Architecture (DynamoDB)
Studied the core concepts and properties of a Data Lake on AWS, including raw data collection, multi-user exploration, and flexible access patterns. Researched serverless data ingestion pipelines using Amazon Kinesis Firehose Delivery Streams and sample data generation via Kinesis Data Generator. Studied AWS Glue serverless ETL architecture: cataloging metadata with Glue Crawlers, managing schemas in the Glue Data Catalog, and generating Python Spark jobs for data transformation. Explored data transformation environments using SageMaker Notebooks to prepare datasets for downstream analysis. Researched ad-hoc SQL queries on S3 using Amazon Athena (ANSI SQL Presto engine) and data visualization architectures using Amazon QuickSight (Data sources, Datasets, Analyses, Visuals, and Dashboards). Researched Amazon DynamoDB features as a fully managed, single-digit millisecond key-value and document database at any scale. Studied NoSQL data modeling best practices, advanced design patterns, and architectural design tradeoffs (such as primary keys and indexes) using the AWS CLI and Console. Researched global serverless database configurations using DynamoDB Global Tables for multi-region replication and low-latency access. Studied Change Data Capture (CDC) mechanisms using DynamoDB Streams integrated with AWS Lambda to build serverless event-driven architectures. Explored modern zero-ETL integration patterns, such as continuously indexing DynamoDB catalogs into Amazon OpenSearch Service to enable natural language queries supported by Amazon Bedrock. AWS Cost Optimization Strategies
Studied AWS Savings Plans (Compute and EC2 Instance types) as flexible pricing models requiring 1- or 3-year hourly commitments with payment options (All, Partial, or No Upfront). Researched Savings Plan Recommendations, lookback periods, queuing/scheduling future plans, and sharing cost savings across AWS Organizations. Compared Standard and Convertible Reserved Instances (RIs) regarding flexibility (instance family, OS, region, AZ modifications), discounts, and standard RI reselling on the RI Marketplace. Researched Amazon RDS Reserved DB Instances, including configuration matching requirements (DB engine, instance class, Multi-AZ) to apply discounts and behaviors when instance sizes auto-scale. Researched EC2 right-sizing using Amazon CloudWatch Agent to monitor custom metrics like memory utilization alongside default metrics (CPU utilization, Network In/Out). Studied CloudWatch Agent deployment on EC2 nodes, including IAM policy configuration (CloudWatchAgentServerPolicy) and configuration wizard setups. Researched Amazon EC2 Resource Optimization (via AWS Cost Explorer) to analyze a 14-day history and recommend optimization actions: Downsizing (underutilized, 1%–40% CPU) or Terminating (idle, <=1% CPU). Understood right-sizing best practices: optimizing before migrating, measuring workloads extensively, starting with non-production environments, and managing burstable instance families (T-types) separately. Cost Visualization & Analytics (AWS Glue & Athena)
Researched cost monitoring and visualization tools including AWS Cost Explorer and AWS Budgets for threshold alerts. Studied data cataloging using AWS Glue Crawlers to catalog Cost and Usage Reports (CUR) stored in Amazon S3. Researched ad-hoc SQL query analysis using Amazon Athena to query and analyze cataloged cost datasets.

title: Worklog
Prerequisite
content:
Prerequisites Requirement Detail AWS account One shared account. Root locked, MFA on. Region ap-southeast-1 (Singapore). us-east-1 only for the CloudFront ACM cert (if a custom domain is used). Team access IAM Identity Center — one SSO user per team member (no shared root, no static keys). AWS CLI Configured via IAM Identity Center (for ECR push, S3 sync). Docker To build and push the 3 service images. 1. Team access with IAM Identity Center The team works on one shared AWS account. Instead of sharing root or handing out access keys, each member gets their own SSO user through IAM Identity Center, grouped and granted access with a permission set.
ℹ️ These SSO users are for humans building the stack (console + CLI). They are separate from the application’s runtime IAM roles, which stay least-privilege — see Security & IAM.
1.1. Open IAM Identity Center Search for and open IAM Identity Center in the console, then enable it.
1.2. Create users and groups Left menu → Users → Add user. Fill the required fields (Username, Email, First name, Last name) → Next to finish. Groups → Create group — name the group and add the users you just created (easier to manage). 1.3. Create a permission set Permission sets → Create permission set. Permission set type = Predefined permission set.
Policy = AdministratorAccess. Set a name and a Session duration, then Create. 📷 [Screenshot] Predefined = AdministratorAccess → /images/5-Workshop/5.2-Prerequisite/iic-permset.png
1.4. Grant access to the AWS account Left menu → under Multi-account permissions, choose AWS accounts.
Tick the AWS account to grant access to → Assign users or groups. Choose the user/group you created → Next. Choose the permission set → Next. Review and Submit. 1.5. Sign in via the AWS access portal Back on the IAM Identity Center Dashboard, open the AWS access portal URL and sign in with the SSO user. 2. Configure the AWS CLI (via IAM Identity Center) Connect the CLI to the access portal so it uses short-lived SSO credentials (no static keys) — needed for ECR push and S3 sync.
aws configure sso # SSO start URL: https://<your-portal>.awsapps.com/start # SSO Region: ap-southeast-1 # (browser opens → approve) → pick account + permission set # CLI default Region: ap-southeast-1 # CLI profile name: saashr # start / refresh a session: aws sso login --profile saashr aws sts get-caller-identity --profile saashr ⚠️ Verify: confirm these CLI steps match what you actually ran (from the earlier setup). If you used a different profile name or method, tell me and I’ll adjust.
3. Docker Install Docker Desktop (or Docker Engine) to build and push the auth / tenant / hr images to ECR in Application Tier.
Step 0 — Cost safety net (AWS Budgets) Set up a budget with email alerts so cost overruns are caught early (free).
Search for and open Billing and Cost Management → Budgets. Click Create budget. Budget setup = Customize (advanced); Budget type = Cost budget – Recommended → Next. Configure the budget:
Budget name: SaaS-HR_Multi-tenant-Budget Period: Custom — Start 2026/06/27, End 2026/07/27 Budgeted amount: 130.00 Scope options: All AWS services (Recommended) Aggregate costs by: Unblended costs Alerts — create 3 alerts at thresholds 60%, 80%, 100% of the budget. Email recipients: team-email-1, team-email-2 Attach actions – Optional → Next. Review and Create. Everything in this workshop bills by the hour (NAT Gateway, RDS, ALB). Tear the stack down when idle — see Clean-up.

title: Proposal
content:
In this section, you need to summarize the contents of the workshop that you plan to conduct.
IoT Weather Platform for Lab Research A Unified AWS Serverless Solution for Real-Time Weather Monitoring 1. Executive Summary The IoT Weather Platform is designed for the ITea Lab team in Ho Chi Minh City to enhance weather data collection and analysis. It supports up to 5 weather stations, with potential scalability to 10-15, utilizing Raspberry Pi edge devices with ESP32 sensors to transmit data via MQTT. The platform leverages AWS Serverless services to deliver real-time monitoring, predictive analytics, and cost efficiency, with access restricted to 5 lab members via Amazon Cognito.
2. Problem Statement What’s the Problem? Current weather stations require manual data collection, becoming unmanageable with multiple units. There is no centralized system for real-time data or analytics, and third-party platforms are costly and overly complex.
The Solution The platform uses AWS IoT Core to ingest MQTT data, AWS Lambda and API Gateway for processing, Amazon S3 for storage (including a data lake), and AWS Glue Crawlers and ETL jobs to extract, transform, and load data from the S3 data lake to another S3 bucket for analysis. AWS Amplify with Next.js provides the web interface, and Amazon Cognito ensures secure access. Similar to Thingsboard and CoreIoT, users can register new devices and manage connections, though this platform operates on a smaller scale and is designed for private use. Key features include real-time dashboards, trend analysis, and low operational costs.
Benefits and Return on Investment The solution establishes a foundational resource for lab members to develop a larger IoT platform, serving as a study resource, and provides a data foundation for AI enthusiasts for model training or analysis. It reduces manual reporting for each station via a centralized platform, simplifying management and maintenance, and improves data reliability. Monthly costs are $0.66 USD per the AWS Pricing Calculator, with a 12-month total of $7.92 USD. All IoT equipment costs are covered by the existing weather station setup, eliminating additional development expenses. The break-even period of 6-12 months is achieved through significant time savings from reduced manual work.
3. Solution Architecture The platform employs a serverless AWS architecture to manage data from 5 Raspberry Pi-based stations, scalable to 15. Data is ingested via AWS IoT Core, stored in an S3 data lake, and processed by AWS Glue Crawlers and ETL jobs to transform and load it into another S3 bucket for analysis. Lambda and API Gateway handle additional processing, while Amplify with Next.js hosts the dashboard, secured by Cognito. The architecture is detailed below:
AWS Services Used AWS IoT Core: Ingests MQTT data from 5 stations, scalable to 15. AWS Lambda: Processes data and triggers Glue jobs (two functions). Amazon API Gateway: Facilitates web app communication. Amazon S3: Stores raw data in a data lake and processed outputs (two buckets). AWS Glue: Crawlers catalog data, and ETL jobs transform and load it. AWS Amplify: Hosts the Next.js web interface. Amazon Cognito: Secures access for lab users. Component Design Edge Devices: Raspberry Pi collects and filters sensor data, sending it to IoT Core. Data Ingestion: AWS IoT Core receives MQTT messages from the edge devices. Data Storage: Raw data is stored in an S3 data lake; processed data is stored in another S3 bucket. Data Processing: AWS Glue Crawlers catalog the data, and ETL jobs transform it for analysis. Web Interface: AWS Amplify hosts a Next.js app for real-time dashboards and analytics. User Management: Amazon Cognito manages user access, allowing up to 5 active accounts. 4. Technical Implementation Implementation Phases This project has two parts—setting up weather edge stations and building the weather platform—each following 4 phases:
Build Theory and Draw Architecture: Research Raspberry Pi setup with ESP32 sensors and design the AWS serverless architecture (1 month pre-internship) Calculate Price and Check Practicality: Use AWS Pricing Calculator to estimate costs and adjust if needed (Month 1). Fix Architecture for Cost or Solution Fit: Tweak the design (e.g., optimize Lambda with Next.js) to stay cost-effective and usable (Month 2). Develop, Test, and Deploy: Code the Raspberry Pi setup, AWS services with CDK/SDK, and Next.js app, then test and release to production (Months 2-3). Technical Requirements
Weather Edge Station: Sensors (temperature, humidity, rainfall, wind speed), a microcontroller (ESP32), and a Raspberry Pi as the edge device. Raspberry Pi runs Raspbian, handles Docker for filtering, and sends 1 MB/day per station via MQTT over Wi-Fi. Weather Platform: Practical knowledge of AWS Amplify (hosting Next.js), Lambda (minimal use due to Next.js), AWS Glue (ETL), S3 (two buckets), IoT Core (gateway and rules), and Cognito (5 users). Use AWS CDK/SDK to code interactions (e.g., IoT Core rules to S3). Next.js reduces Lambda workload for the fullstack web app. 5. Timeline & Milestones Project Timeline
Pre-Internship (Month 0): 1 month for planning and old station review. Internship (Months 1-3): 3 months. Month 1: Study AWS and upgrade hardware. Month 2: Design and adjust architecture. Month 3: Implement, test, and launch. Post-Launch: Up to 1 year for research. 6. Budget Estimation You can find the budget estimation on the AWS Pricing Calculator.
Or you can download the Budget Estimation File.
Infrastructure Costs AWS Services: AWS Lambda: $0.00/month (1,000 requests, 512 MB storage). S3 Standard: $0.15/month (6 GB, 2,100 requests, 1 GB scanned). Data Transfer: $0.02/month (1 GB inbound, 1 GB outbound). AWS Amplify: $0.35/month (256 MB, 500 ms requests). Amazon API Gateway: $0.01/month (2,000 requests). AWS Glue ETL Jobs: $0.02/month (2 DPUs). AWS Glue Crawlers: $0.07/month (1 crawler). MQTT (IoT Core): $0.08/month (5 devices, 45,000 messages). Total: $0.7/month, $8.40/12 months
Hardware: $265 one-time (Raspberry Pi 5 and sensors). 7. Risk Assessment Risk Matrix Network Outages: Medium impact, medium probability. Sensor Failures: High impact, low probability. Cost Overruns: Medium impact, low probability. Mitigation Strategies Network: Local storage on Raspberry Pi with Docker. Sensors: Regular checks and spares. Cost: AWS budget alerts and optimization. Contingency Plans Revert to manual methods if AWS fails. Use CloudFormation for cost-related rollbacks. 8. Expected Outcomes Technical Improvements: Real-time data and analytics replace manual processes.
Scalable to 10-15 stations.
Long-term Value 1-year data foundation for AI research.
Reusable for future projects.

title: Networking
content:
Build the network foundation first: one VPC, 6 subnets across 2 AZ, an Internet Gateway, one NAT Gateway, and the security groups.
Step 1 — VPC + 6 Subnets + IGW + NAT VPC → Create VPC — name saashr-vpc, CIDR 10.0.0.0/16. VPC -> Created VPC 2. Subnets — create 6:
Subnet name AZ CIDR Purpose public-1a 1a 10.0.1.0/24 ALB, NAT public-1b 1b 10.0.2.0/24 ALB app-1a 1a 10.0.11.0/24 ECS Fargate app-1b 1b 10.0.12.0/24 ECS Fargate data-1a 1a 10.0.21.0/24 RDS Primary data-1b 1b 10.0.22.0/24 RDS Standby VPC -> Subnets -> Create Subnet 6 subnets create successfully Internet Gateway — create saashr-igw, attach to VPC. VPC -> Internet gateway -> create internet gateway Actions -> Attach to VPC
NAT Gateway — create 1 in public-1a, allocate an Elastic IP. Choose VPC -> Elastic IP addresses -> Allocate Elastic IP Address Click Allocate
Click VPC -> NAT gateway -> Create NAT gateway Creation complete Route tables: rt-public → 0.0.0.0/0 → IGW → associate public-1a, public-1b. rt-app → 0.0.0.0/0 → NAT Gateway → associate app-1a, app-1b. rt-data → no internet route (local only) → associate data-1a, data-1b. Click VPC -> route table -> create route table Click Actions -> Edit subnet associations Click Edit routes 3 route tables created successfully
Step 2 — Security Groups (zero-trust) SG Ingress Egress sg-alb 80/443 from 0.0.0.0/0 all sg-ecs app port (8000–8002) from sg-alb only all (NAT → Cognito/SQS/ECR/CloudWatch) sg-rds 3306 from sg-ecs only none The 3 security groups
ALB security group inbound and outbound ECS security group inbound and outbound RDS security group inbound and outbound Chained SGs (alb → ecs → rds) mean the database can never be reached from the internet — only from the ECS tasks. See Security & IAM.

title: Blogs Posted

title: Data & Identity
content:
The data tier (RDS), the secrets store (SSM), and the identity setup (Cognito pool + the app’s RS256 JWT keys).
Step 3 — RDS MySQL Multi-AZ RDS → Subnet groups → saashr-db-subnets = data-1a + data-1b. Create database → MySQL → db.t4g.micro → Multi-AZ: Yes (create a standby). Search Aurora and RDS -> Databases -> Click Create Database -> Choose Full Configuration 3. Storage type: gp3; Storage 20 GB gp3; Enable Storage Autoscaling: Untick. 4. VPC = saashr-vpc, subnet group = saashr-db-subnets, Public access = No, SG = sg-rds. Database create successfully After creation, run database/init.sql to create auth_db, tenant_db, hr_db (via a temporary bastion). create ec2 Cut the init.sql file into ec2 Connect ec2 to database 📎 Attachment: database/init.sql (place under 5.4-Data-Identity/files/).
Step 4 — Secrets (SSM Parameter Store) Store configuration and secrets in Systems Manager → Parameter Store as SecureString (free), later referenced by the ECS task definition — nothing is hard-coded in the images.
Search for and open Systems Manager → Parameter Store. Click Create parameter. 3. For each parameter below: enter the Name, set Type = SecureString, paste the Value, then Create parameter. Repeat for all 7. Parameter name Holds /saashr/cognito/client_id Cognito app client ID /saashr/cognito/pool_id Cognito user pool ID /saashr/db/host RDS endpoint (host) /saashr/db/password RDS database password /saashr/jwt/private_key RS256 JWT signing private key /saashr/jwt/public_key RS256 JWT verification public key /saashr/sqs/url SQS queue URL On AWS Console -> click Cloudshell at the bottom -> patse this command to get private key and public key Generating the RS256 JWT keypair /saashr/jwt/private_key and /saashr/jwt/public_key are an RSA keypair. Generate them once in AWS CloudShell, then paste the PEM output into the two SecureString parameters above:
python3 -c " from cryptography.hazmat.primitives.asymmetric import rsa from cryptography.hazmat.primitives import serialization key = rsa.generate_private_key(public_exponent=65537, key_size=2048) priv = key.private_bytes(serialization.Encoding.PEM, serialization.PrivateFormat.TraditionalOpenSSL, serialization.NoEncryption()).decode() pub = key.public_key().public_bytes(serialization.Encoding.PEM, serialization.PublicFormat.SubjectPublicKeyInfo).decode() print('=== PRIVATE KEY ===') print(priv) print('=== PUBLIC KEY ===') print(pub) " ⚠️ Never publish the real private key. Show only this command — redact the printed key and mask the /saashr/jwt/private_key value in any screenshot. Anyone holding the private key can forge JWTs for the app.
db/password and jwt/private_key must be SecureString. The same values can be set from the CLI, e.g. aws ssm put-parameter --name "/saashr/db/host" --type SecureString --value "<rds-endpoint>".
Step 5 — Cognito Search for and open Cognito → User pools.
Click Create user pool and configure:
Application type: Traditional web application Name your application: saashr-app Sign-in identifiers: Email Self-registration: Enable self-registration Return URL: https://localhost (dev; use the CloudFront URL in production) Then click Create user directory to create the pool.
Open the new user pool → App clients.
Click Create app client:
Application type: Traditional web application Name your application: saashr-app Return URL: https://localhost Then click Create app client to finish.
Note the User pool ID and App client ID → store them in SSM as /saashr/cognito/pool_id and /saashr/cognito/client_id (Step 4).
This pool provides the user directory and login. Tenant isolation (tenant_id) and roles are carried in the application’s RS256 JWT (keys from Step 4).

title: Async Messaging (SQS)
content:
tenant-service publishes business events; hr-service consumes them asynchronously. The queue decouples the two and absorbs spikes; a Dead-Letter Queue catches poison messages.
Step 6 — SQS + DLQ Search for and open Simple Queue Service → Queues. Create the main queue saashr-events: Type: Standard Name: saashr-events Keep the default configuration, but set Receive message wait time = 20 (enables long-polling — matches the consumer). Access policy: Advanced Dead-letter queue → “Set this queue to receive undeliverable messages” = Enabled Choose queue: arn:aws:sqs:ap-southeast-1:<account-id>:saashr-events-dlq Maximum receives: 5 Click Create queue. 3. Create the dead-letter queue saashr-events-dlq:
Type: Standard Name: saashr-events-dlq Access policy: Advanced Click Create queue. Note the saashr-events Queue URL → save into SSM as /saashr/sqs/url (Step 4). The console only lets you select a DLQ that already exists. If so, create saashr-events-dlq first, then enable the redrive policy on saashr-events.
Application code (already implemented) Publisher — tenant-service/app/core/sqs.py (boto3, uses the ECS task-role credentials, no static keys):
# send_message(QueueUrl=SQS_QUEUE_URL, MessageBody=json.dumps(event)) Consumer — hr-service/app/core/worker.py (start_sqs_consumer): long-poll → process → delete_message on success (failures retry, then land in the DLQ).
📎 Attachment: sqs.py, worker.py (place under 5.5-Async-SQS/files/).
Event-driven decoupling via SQS is the scalability story for the async path — see also Security & IAM for the task-role SQS permissions.

title: Workshop
content:
SaaS HR Multi-Tenant on AWS Overview A multi-tenant HR SaaS (FastAPI microservices + React) deployed on AWS as a 3-tier architecture: CloudFront + S3 for the frontend, ECS Fargate behind an Application Load Balancer for the API, and RDS MySQL Multi-AZ for data. Async events use Amazon SQS, identity uses Amazon Cognito, monitoring uses CloudWatch + SNS. Built by hand in the AWS Console in ap-southeast-1.
Each customer company is an isolated tenant — data is separated per tenant.
This workshop uses more than 3 AWS services (Cognito, SQS, RDS, ECS Fargate, ALB, S3, CloudFront, CloudWatch, SNS, SSM) and covers architecture design, end-to-end deployment, testing, and clean-up.
Content Overview & Architecture Prerequisite Networking Data & Identity Async Messaging (SQS) Application Tier (ECS) Frontend & CDN Monitoring & Alerts Testing & Validation Security & IAM Clean-up

title: Application Tier (ECS)
content:
Push the images to ECR, put an ALB in front, then run the 3 services on ECS Fargate with auto scaling.
Step 8 — ECR + push images Search ECR → choose Private registry -> Repositories -> Create Repository: create 3 repos: saashr-auth, saashr-tenant, saashr-hr. 3 repositories created successfully. 2. Build and push each (example for auth):
aws ecr get-login-password --region ap-southeast-1 \ | docker login --username AWS --password-stdin <acct>.dkr.ecr.ap-southeast-1.amazonaws.com docker build -t saashr-auth ./microservices/auth-service docker tag saashr-auth:latest <acct>.dkr.ecr.ap-southeast-1.amazonaws.com/saashr-auth:latest docker push <acct>.dkr.ecr.ap-southeast-1.amazonaws.com/saashr-auth:latest Login aws cli Push from cli (hr’s push): 3 ECR repos with a pushed image tag 📎 Attachment: the three Dockerfiles (place under 5.6-Compute-ECS/files/).
Step 9 — Application Load Balancer Target groups (type IP, one per service): tg-auth(port 8000), tg-tenant (port 8001), tg-hr(port 8002); Health check /api/v1/{service}/health. Healthy thresold = 2 (60s -> deploying faster) Continue with tg-tenant and tg-hr Search EC2 → Load Balancers → Create ALB saashr-alb, internet-facing, subnets = public-1a + public-1b, SG = sg-alb. Select Application Load balancer -> create Name: saashr-alb Choose: internet facing ipv4 VPC: saashr-vpc subnets = public-1a + public-1b Listeners and routing: Choose Forward to target groups protocol: HTTP Port: 80 Target group: tg-auth -> Create ALB created successfully Listener :80 rules → /api/v1/auth/*→tg-auth, /api/v1/tenants/*→tg-tenant, /api/v1/hr/*→tg-hr. Then click to HTTP:80 Click Add Rule Conditions -> Path: /api/v1/auth/* Target group: tg-auth Continue with 3 rules left. Step 10 — ECS Fargate + Auto Scaling ECS → Create cluster saashr-cluster (Fargate). Task definitions (one per service, Fargate, 0.25 vCPU / 0.5 GB):
container image = ECR repo, port = app port.
Task role (least privilege): sqs:SendMessage/ReceiveMessage/DeleteMessage (tenant/hr), cognito-idp:* minimal (auth), ssm:GetParameters.
Execution role: ECR pull + CloudWatch Logs + SSM read.
Secrets from SSM; log driver awslogs → /ecs/saashr/{service}. 1.1. Create task definition for auth:
Name: saashr-auth
CPU: .25 vCPU
Memory: .5 GB task role: None
task execution role: ecsTaskExecutionRole
Container name: saashr-auth
Image URI: copy from auth repository in ECR repositories
port: 8000
Environment variables -> Click Add environment variable and select/paste infomation from SSM parameter store as this picture: 1.2. Create task definition for tenant: name: saashr-tenant CPU: .25 vCPU Memory: .5 GB task role: create new role Role name: saashr-tenant-task-role Policy -> Create new policy Replace s3:GetObject with sqs:SendMessage and “arn:aws:s3:::[[readS3Paths]]/*” with "arn:aws:sqs:ap-southeast-1:016461465939:saashr-events" Click Create role task role: saashr-tenant-task-role task execution role: ecsTaskExecutionRole Container name: saashr-auth Image URI: copy from tenant repository in ECR repositories port: 8001 Environment variables -> Click Add environment variable and select/paste infomation from SSM parameter store as this picture: 1.3. Create task definition for hr: name: saashr-hr CPU: .25 vCPU Memory: .5 GB task role: create new role Role name: saashr-tenant-task-role Policy -> Create new policy Replace “s3:GetObject” with "sqs:ReceiveMessage","sqs:DeleteMessage","sqs:GetQueueAttributes" and “arn:aws:s3:::[[readS3Paths]]/*” with "arn:aws:sqs:ap-southeast-1:016461465939:saashr-events" Click Create role task role: saashr-hr-task-role task execution role: ecsTaskExecutionRole Container name: saashr-auth Image URI: copy from tenant repository in ECR repositories port: 8002 Environment variables -> Click Add environment variable and select/paste infomation from SSM parameter store as this picture: Services (one per task def): launch type Fargate, subnets = app-1a + app-1b, SG = sg-ecs, assign public IP = DISABLED, desired count 2, attach to the matching target group. title: Monitoring & Alerts
content:
Goal: monitor the ECS services — when a service’s CPU Utilization reaches ≥ 70%, CloudWatch triggers an alarm and emails the team through Amazon SNS.
Step 7 — Amazon SNS (topic + email subscription) 7.1 Create the SNS topic Amazon SNS → Topics → Create topic.
Type: Standard Name: saashr-alerts Display name: saashr-alerts Click Create topic. 7.2 Create the email subscription Open the saashr-alerts topic → Subscriptions tab → Create subscription.
Protocol: Email Endpoint: <team-email> Click Create subscription. 7.3 Confirm the email Amazon SNS sends a confirmation email to that address. Open it and click Confirm subscription. The status changes from Pending confirmation to Confirmed.
Step 13 — CloudWatch Alarm → SNS 13.1 Create alarm → select metric CloudWatch → Alarms → Create alarm → Select metric.
13.2 Choose the ECS metric In Select metric, follow ECS → ClusterName, ServiceName:
Cluster: saashr-cluster2 Service: saashr-hr-svc Metric: CPUUtilization Click Select metric. 13.3 Configure the condition Statistic: Average Period: 1 minute Threshold type: Static Whenever CPUUtilization is: Greater/Equal Threshold value: 70 The alarm enters the ALARM state when CPU Utilization is ≥ 70% over a 1-minute period.
13.4 Configure the action Alarm state trigger: In alarm Notification → Select an existing SNS topic → saashr-alerts CloudWatch emails every confirmed subscriber of the topic when the alarm fires.
13.5 Name the alarm Alarm name: saashr-alerts-alarm-noti (add a description if needed) Click Next → Create alarm. 13.6 Verify The new alarm appears with:
Metric: CPUUtilization · Threshold: ≥ 70% · Action: SNS Notification State: OK (or Insufficient data at first) Open it to watch the CPU Utilization graph, the threshold line, and the alarm history.
This alarm watches one service (saashr-hr-svc). Repeat 13.1–13.5 for auth and tenant to cover all three.
Logs Each service also streams logs to the CloudWatch Logs group /ecs/saashr/{service} (configured in the ECS task definition, Step 10).

title: Testing & Validation
content:
End-to-end validation of the deployed stack via the CloudFront entry point, followed by error testing, high-availability testing, and observability. Base URL:
BASE="https://d3htvmot332c6v.cloudfront.net/" 1. Health checks (smoke test) curl -s $BASE/api/v1/auth/health curl -s $BASE/api/v1/tenants/health curl -s $BASE/api/v1/hr/health Result — all three healthy (the database:up fields confirm ECS and RDS are reachable):
{"status":"healthy","service":"auth-service"} {"status":"healthy","database":"up","service":"tenant-service"} {"status":"healthy","database":"up","service":"hr-service"} 2. Frontend delivery curl -I $BASE/ Result: HTTP/2 200, content-type: text/html — the React SPA is served through CloudFront (from the private S3 origin).
3. Authentication — end-to-end # Register a workspace + owner curl -s -X POST $BASE/api/v1/auth/register -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"email":"owner@acmedemo.com","password":"Passw0rd!","tenant_name":"Acme Demo","subdomain":"acmedemo"}' # Log in → receive an access token curl -s -X POST $BASE/api/v1/auth/login -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"email":"owner@acmedemo.com","password":"Passw0rd!","subdomain":"acmedemo"}' # Verify the token curl -s $BASE/api/v1/auth/me -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" Result — /me returns the authenticated identity with the correct role:
{"id":"usr_9476d71e-...","email":"owner@acmedemo.com","status":"active","active_tenant_id":"tenant_369f39d7-...","role":"owner"} The token is an app-signed RS256 JWT (auth-service signs with the private key; the other services verify with the public key — see Data & Identity). Its claims (tenant_id, role) drive every downstream authorization check.
4. Business operations (authenticated, tenant-scoped) curl -s $BASE/api/v1/tenants/my-tenant -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" curl -s -X POST $BASE/api/v1/hr/departments -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"name":"Engineering"}' curl -s $BASE/api/v1/hr/employees -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" Result:
{"id":"tenant_369f39d7-...","name":"Acme Demo","subdomain":"acmedemo","status":"active","plan_tier":"basic","created_at":"2026-07-08T00:24:15"} {"id":"dept_bcd98e2a-...","tenant_id":"tenant_369f39d7-...","name":"Engineering","description":null,"created_at":"2026-07-08T00:27:25"} [] Reading the tenant, creating a department (201), and listing employees (empty for a new tenant) all succeed — data reads/writes to RDS work through the authenticated path.
5. Validation & error testing API error responses are tested directly against the ALB (http://<alb-dns>/...). CloudFront’s SPA custom error responses map 403/404 to index.html (200), which masks API error codes for browser clients — so the API tier is validated at the ALB, where server: uvicorn confirms the request reached the application.
Test Command Observed result No token curl -i $ALB/api/v1/hr/employees 403 Forbidden {"detail":"Not authenticated"} ✅ Wrong password login with a bad password 401 Unauthorized {"detail":"Invalid email or password"} ✅ Duplicate subdomain register an existing subdomain 409 {"detail":"Tenant subdomain already registered."} ✅ Duplicate department create a department with an existing name Blocked by the DB unique constraint uq_tenant_dept (see note) Tenant isolation register tenant B, log in as B, GET /api/v1/hr/employees Returns only B’s data — A’s records are never visible Observed — duplicate subdomain is rejected:
{"detail":"Tenant subdomain already registered."} Known limitation: a duplicate department name is correctly prevented at the database level (unique key uq_tenant_dept), but the API currently surfaces it as HTTP 500 instead of a clean 409 Conflict. The data integrity is safe; only the error response needs improving.
6. Async messaging (SQS) — testing finding End-to-end testing of the asynchronous path (tenant status change → SQS → hr-service) surfaced an integration issue in the current deployment, which is exactly what this stage of testing is meant to catch:
Infrastructure is healthy — a message sent manually to saashr-events was accepted (the NumberOfMessagesSent / SentMessageSize metrics increased), confirming the queue and DLQ are correctly provisioned. Producer — tenant-service did not publish to the queue on a status change (no MessagesSent from the app), despite the task role holding sqs:SendMessage. Consumer — the queued test message was not drained (MessagesVisible = 1, MessagesDeleted = 0), so the consumer was not processing during the test window. Assessment: the event-driven design (SQS decoupling + DLQ + long-polling consumer, see Async Messaging) is sound; the gap is in the deployment wiring (likely a stale service image and/or consumer startup), and the fix is in progress. This is recorded here as an honest testing finding rather than claimed as passing.
7. Observability Logs: CloudWatch Logs groups /ecs/saashr-auth, /ecs/saashr-tenant, /ecs/saashr-hr capture request logs and unhandled errors. Metrics: CloudWatch → ECS CPUUtilization per service. Alert → email: trigger the alarm without generating real load: aws cloudwatch set-alarm-state --alarm-name saashr-alerts-alarm-noti \ --state-value ALARM --state-reason "manual test" Expected: the SNS email arrives within seconds and the alarm shows the ALARM state (see Monitoring & Alerts).

title: Security & IAM
content:
How the design applies least privilege, keeps resources private, and avoids hard-coded credentials.
Principle of Least Privilege Each ECS service assumes its own task role with only the actions it needs:
Service Allowed actions tenant sqs:SendMessage on saashr-events hr sqs:ReceiveMessage, sqs:DeleteMessage on saashr-events auth minimal cognito-idp (InitiateAuth), ssm:GetParameters all ssm:GetParameters (own params), CloudWatch Logs write Example — the tenant task role (scoped to one queue, one action):
{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [{ "Effect": "Allow", "Action": "sqs:SendMessage", "Resource": "arn:aws:sqs:ap-southeast-1:<acct>:saashr-events" }] } The task role with its inline least-privilege policy → No hard-coded credentials boto3 uses the ECS task-role credentials at runtime — no static access keys in code or images. Secrets (DB password, SQS URL, Cognito IDs) come from SSM Parameter Store at task start, not from the repo. Private by default ECS and RDS run in private subnets with no public IP. S3 blocks all public access; served only via CloudFront OAC. RDS data subnets have no route to the internet. Zero-trust security groups sg-alb → sg-ecs → sg-rds are chained so each tier only accepts traffic from the tier in front (see Networking). The database is unreachable from the internet.
📷 [Screenshot] S3 “Block all public access = On” + RDS “Publicly accessible = No” → /images/5-Workshop/5.10-Security-IAM/private-resources.png

title: Clean-up
content:
Delete resources in reverse dependency order to avoid ongoing charges. The NAT Gateway, RDS, and ALB bill by the hour — remove these first if you only want to pause.
Teardown order CloudFront — disable, then delete the distribution. S3 — empty, then delete saashr-frontend-<acct>. ALB — delete the load balancer + the 3 target groups. ECS — set services to desired 0, delete the services, then the cluster. NAT Gateway — delete (stops the biggest hourly charge). RDS — delete the database instance (skip or take a final snapshot). VPC endpoints (if any) — delete. Elastic IP — release the NAT’s EIP. VPC — delete (removes subnets, route tables, IGW, security groups). Then remove the standalone services: 10. SNS topic + CloudWatch alarms + log groups. 11. ECR repositories (delete images first). 12. Cognito user pool, SSM parameters, ACM cert (if a custom domain was used).
Double-check the Billing → Budgets page a day later to confirm charges have stopped. Leftover NAT Gateways and RDS instances are the usual surprise bills.
